2025 · arXiv / NeurIPS 2025 D&B · Benchmarks & Evals

QuestBench: Can LLMs ask the right question to acquire information in reasoning tasks?

Real queries are often underspecified — the answer is unreachable until you ask for the one missing fact. QuestBench formalizes this as a constraint-satisfaction problem and tests whether models can name that fact. Frontier LLMs that ace well-specified math fall to 40–50% on logic and planning.

Belinda Z. Li, Been Kim, Zi Wang · Google DeepMind & MIT
venue arXiv (NeurIPS 2025 D&B) citations 40 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of June 2026
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

Reasoning benchmarks — GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval — all share a hidden assumption: the problem comes fully specified. Every number you need is on the page; your job is just to compute. But real users don't work that way. They leave out the size of the flock, the contents of a meal, the position of a block. A capable assistant has to notice the hole and ask for the one piece of information that closes it — not guess, not hallucinate a value, not solve a different problem.

Prior work on clarifying questions mostly lives in subjective territory: task-oriented dialogue, preference elicitation, ambiguous open-domain QA. There, multiple clarifying questions are reasonable and the "right" one depends on context and user taste — which makes objective scoring almost impossible. QuestBench's move is to carve off a slice of the problem where there is a provably correct question, so you can measure information-gathering the way you'd measure accuracy.

The key conceptual distinction the paper insists on is underspecification vs. ambiguity. Ambiguity means a query has several valid readings, each of which is solvable. Underspecification means the query — under its intended reading — is simply not solvable without more information. Children resolve underspecification constantly by asking questions; the paper asks whether LLMs, which now top math and logic leaderboards, can do the same when the necessary fact is withheld.

// how it works

The method

QuestBench models a reasoning task as a constraint satisfaction problem (CSP) — a tuple ⟨X, D, C, A, y⟩ of variables, domains, constraints, known assignments, and a target variable y. A value is Known if it is directly assigned or derivable from what's assigned via the constraints. The benchmark's central definition is clean: a CSP is underspecified exactly when the constraints plus the known assignments still don't pin down the target.

C(x) ∧ A  ⟹  ¬ Known(y) // underspecified ⇔ even with all constraints and known facts, the target stays unknown

To make scoring tractable, the authors restrict to the simplest non-trivial case: 1-sufficient CSPs, where the smallest set of unknowns that would unlock the target has size exactly one. There is a single fact whose value is necessary and sufficient — so "the right question" is well-defined. (Asking about any variable that qualifies counts as correct.) 1-sufficient problems are framed as multiple choice: the model is shown the underspecified problem and a list of candidate questions — including the decoy "No questions needed." — and must pick a sufficient one. That recasts an open-ended skill as a clean classification, and it's a deliberate upper bound: a k-sufficient task becomes 1-sufficient after k−1 questions, so performance here approximates a ceiling on information-gathering.

Four domains instantiate the framework, spanning math, logic, and planning:

  • GSM-Q / GSME-Q — grade-school math built from GSM-Plus's "distractor" variant of GSM8K. Human annotators translate each word problem into a CSP, then a required assignment is withheld (along with the distractors), making exactly one question necessary. GSM-Q is the verbal form; GSME-Q is the same problem written as equations.
  • Logic-Q — propositional logic from SimpleLogic ("If Alice is jittery and worried, then Alice is pleasant…"). The original known facts are discarded and rebuilt by backward search so that a single missing property determines the target proposition's truth.
  • Planning-Q — Blocks World PDDL problems with a partially observed initial state, where one extra observation (e.g. "is block d on block b?") is needed to disambiguate the shortest plan to the goal.

Crucially, each problem carries an objective difficulty profile from two reference solvers (brute-force forward search and backward search): the number of variables |X|, the number of constraints |C|, the backward-search depth d, and 𝔼BF, the expected number of random guesses to hit a sufficient variable.

𝔼BF = |X| + 1𝔰 + 1 // 𝔰 = number of sufficient variables; guesses drawn without replacement

The full benchmark is large — 1,150 Logic-Q, 7,500 Planning-Q, 23,642 GSM-Q, and 6,590 GSME-Q tasks — though the math domains are evaluated on representative subsets (288 and 151) to concentrate budget on the harder domains. Annotation used 21 fluent-English annotators from five countries, paid $27–$55/hour.

// what they found

The results

The headline is a sharp split by domain. On the math tasks, the frontier models essentially saturate: o1-preview hits 98.01% on GSME-Q and 91.32% on GSM-Q, and most strong models clear 80–96%. But on Logic-Q and Planning-Q, no model exceeds ~50%. The best Logic-Q score is o1-preview at 49.91% (zero-shot); the best Planning-Q is Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking at 47.32%. Claude 3.5 Sonnet collapses to 7.53% on Planning-Q. Neither chain-of-thought nor four-shot prompting moves the needle meaningfully on the two hard domains.

Model (zero-shot)Logic-QPlanning-QGSME-QGSM-Q
o1-preview49.9142.3198.0191.32
Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking37.3947.3296.6984.72
Claude 3.5 Sonnet30.097.5394.7091.32
GPT-4o27.3924.4996.6986.81
Gemini 1.5 Pro29.1319.8048.3477.78
BFS (depth 10)99.893.9100100

The bottom row matters: a plain breadth-first search to depth 10 solves 99.8% of Logic-Q and 93.9% of Planning-Q. The tasks are not unsolvable or ill-posed — they're algorithmically easy. The models are failing a search/verification problem that a few lines of code dispatch.

Why do they fail? The paper's most important experiment isolates question-asking from raw reasoning. They build a well-specified variant of every task (restoring the missing fact), keep only the instances each model can actually solve, and re-measure question-asking on that filtered set. If asking were merely downstream of solving, accuracy should jump. It doesn't: on Logic-Q the change is essentially zero (Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking and GPT-4o both move 0.0%), and Planning-Q rises only a few points. Models that can solve the puzzle still can't identify which fact is missing — knowing how to reason is necessary but not sufficient for knowing what to ask.

A second probe asks whether models can even detect underspecification — given a mix of solvable and underspecified problems, say "not sure" when a fact is missing. Reported as F1, most models struggle, and on Planning-Q they fall below a random proportional baseline (F1 ≈ 41.75): Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking scores 0.82, GPT-4o 1.23, Gemini 1.5 Flash 0.00. They almost never flag the gap — they barrel ahead and answer. Two smaller patterns round out the picture: verbal GSM-Q is consistently harder than its symbolic GSME-Q twin (models reason better over equations than prose), and a GPT-4o sanity check on the full math sets (99.39% GSME-Q, 84.05% GSM-Q) confirms the subset numbers aren't a sampling artifact.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

The core finding is robust and well-instrumented — the filtered "can-solve-but-can't-ask" ablation is exactly the control that makes "LLMs can't ask the right question" more than a vibe, and the BFS baseline forecloses the "the tasks are just impossible" rebuttal. But three things temper how far the headline travels.

The model snapshot is the biggest caveat. Experiments ran 06/2024–03/2025, so the frontier tops out at o1-preview and Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking Experimental. The paper's negative claim is therefore about early-2025 reasoning models — there is no o3/o4, Gemini 2.5, Claude 3.7/4, or DeepSeek-R1 in the main tables (the v2 revision only adds a handful of qualitative Gemini 2.5 Pro examples in an appendix). Since CoT and few-shot barely helped, it's plausible that heavier inference-time reasoning is precisely what moves Logic-Q/Planning-Q — which would make "40–50%" a fact about a model generation rather than a durable property of LLMs. To its credit, the filtered ablation argues the deficit isn't pure reasoning horsepower, but that argument also predates the strongest reasoning models.

Multiple-choice recognition flatters the models, and even so they fail. Picking a sufficient variable from a list is much easier than generating a clarifying question from scratch, and the authors are explicit that 1-sufficient performance is an upper bound. That cuts two ways: the low scores are genuinely damning (they fail the easy version), but the absolute numbers can't be read as a deployment estimate — a real agent must generate, sequence, and ground questions over multiple turns, none of which this single-question, closed-set format tests.

Realism is inverted relative to the conclusion. The one natural-language domain — GSM-Q, derived from GSM8K via GSM-Plus — is the one models saturate, and the authors themselves note math may be "specifically studied and optimized" (read: a contamination-and-training-emphasis confound that makes the math ceiling hard to interpret). The failures concentrate in the two synthetic, templated domains: SimpleLogic's "Alice" rules and BlocksWorld. Those are clean for scoring but a step removed from the messy underspecified user queries that motivate the paper, so the leap from "fails on templated logic/planning" to "can't ask users the right question" is an extrapolation the benchmark doesn't directly close.

baselines

Strong where it counts: BFS at depths 3/5/10 brackets task difficulty and shows depth-10 search solves 99.8% (Logic-Q) / 93.9% (Planning-Q), so failures are model-side, not task-side. The filtered well-specified ablation is the standout control. Missing: a generation baseline and current reasoning models.

contamination

GSM-Q/GSME-Q inherit GSM8K lineage; saturation on math is confounded by likely training exposure (authors flag this). The hard domains are synthetic and templated, so less contamination-prone — but templating invites format-overfitting instead.

what's missed

Closed-set, single-question, single-turn. No open-ended question generation, no k-sufficient multi-turn dialogue, no user simulation. "Any sufficient variable is correct" is permissive, and the "No questions needed" option folds detection into the same multiple-choice.

would raise confidence

Re-run with o3/Gemini 2.5/Claude 4/R1-class models; an open-generation variant scored against the sufficient-variable set; a k-sufficient sequential track; and a non-templated, naturalistic logic/planning split to rule out format effects and test the real-world claim.

// bottom line

A genuinely clean formalization of an underexplored skill, with the right control baked in: models that can solve a problem still can't reliably name the one missing fact, and on planning they don't even notice it's missing (sub-random detection F1). The result is real. Just hold it at its actual altitude — a tight, closed-set, single-question probe on early-2025 frontier models, where the saturated domain is the realistic one and the failing domains are synthetic. It proves the gap exists; it doesn't yet tell you how wide it is for a 2026 agent generating questions in the wild.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Underspecification is a distinct, measurable skill. By casting reasoning as a CSP and isolating the case where exactly one fact is missing, QuestBench turns "ask the right clarifying question" into something with a provable ground truth and clean multiple-choice scoring.
  2. Solving ≠ asking. Filtering to problems each model can actually solve barely changes its question-asking accuracy (≈0% on Logic-Q). Reasoning competence on well-specified problems does not transfer to identifying what information is absent.
  3. The failure is concrete and snapshot-bound. Early-2025 frontier models saturate math (up to 98%) but sit at 40–50% on logic/planning — tasks a depth-10 BFS solves ~94–100% — and on planning they score below random at even detecting underspecification. Whether stronger 2026 reasoning models close the gap is the open question the main tables can't answer.
